name: Family-Forced Career Stream
description: Parents or family insisting on a career path (engineering, medicine, etc.) that doesn't align with personal interests
example_situations: Parents want engineering, I want arts
Dad says only doctor/engineer is real career
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna was born into a warrior family and expected to fight — his crisis began when his duty conflicted with his heart

name: Career Confusion
description: Complete lack of clarity about what to do professionally
example_situations: I don't know what to do with my life
Everyone has a plan except me
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna stood paralyzed between two armies, unable to act — the same paralysis of not knowing what to do

name: Career Switch Fear
description: Fear of leaving a stable job/career for something that aligns better with passion or purpose
example_situations: Should I leave my stable job for a startup?
Want to switch from IT to teaching but scared
Corporate job pays well but soul is dying
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna had to choose between comfortable inaction and risky but righteous action

name: Job Loss
description: Recently lost a job — dealing with financial stress, identity crisis, and shame
example_situations: Fired and don't know how to tell family
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna lost everything he knew on the battlefield — his certainty, his role, his confidence

name: Career Burnout
description: Exhaustion and loss of motivation from overwork
example_situations: Working 14 hours daily, can't take it anymore
Hate my job but can't quit
arjuna_parallel: Even the greatest warrior can be overwhelmed — Arjuna's vishada (dejection) in Chapter 1 is burnout of the soul

name: Impostor Syndrome
description: Feeling undeserving of success, fear of being exposed as a fraud
example_situations: Got promoted but feel like I don't deserve it
Everyone thinks I'm smart but I'm faking it
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna doubted his own worthiness as a warrior despite being the greatest archer alive

name: Love Marriage Opposition
description: Family opposing choice of partner — interfaith, intercaste, or simply not approved
example_situations: Parents won't accept my partner's religion
Different caste, family threatening to disown
Love marriage but parents want arranged
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna married Subhadra against convention — love and duty can coexist

name: Breakup Grief
description: Heartbreak and inability to move on after a relationship ends
example_situations: It's been months but still crying
They moved on but I'm stuck
arjuna_parallel: Attachment to what was — Krishna teaches that clinging to the past prevents growth

name: Marital Conflict
description: Ongoing fights and disconnect with spouse
arjuna_parallel: The Mahabharata itself is about family conflict — the Gita was spoken in the midst of the greatest family war

name: Parental Expectations
description: Crushing weight of parental expectations — academic, career, marriage, lifestyle
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna's crisis was partly about living up to his lineage — the weight of being a Pandava

name: In-Law Conflict
description: Conflict with in-laws, especially mother-in-law/daughter-in-law dynamics
arjuna_parallel: The Pandavas lived with complex family dynamics — Kunti, Draupadi, and the five brothers navigated constant family tension

name: Deep Loneliness
description: Profound sense of being alone despite having people around
example_situations: Surrounded by people but deeply alone
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna stood alone between two armies — the loneliness of having to face your truth with nobody who fully understands

name: Death of Loved One
description: Grieving the loss of someone close — parent, spouse, child, friend
example_situations: Mother passed away, world feels empty
arjuna_parallel: Krishna's teaching begins with Arjuna's grief about the impending death of his loved ones — this is THE original use case of the Gita

name: Chronic Illness
description: Living with ongoing health condition that limits life
arjuna_parallel: The body is described as kshetra (field) in Chapter 13 — knowing the difference between the body and the self is key

name: Death Anxiety
description: Fear of one's own death or mortality
example_situations: Health scare made me fear mortality
arjuna_parallel: Chapter 2 directly addresses this: 'The soul is never born and never dies' — Krishna's first major teaching

name: Existential Crisis
description: Questioning the meaning and purpose of life itself
example_situations: What's the point of all this?
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna's very first question is existential: 'What good is a kingdom if those I love are dead?' (1.32-35)

name: Quarter-Life Crisis
description: Feeling behind in life in your mid-20s, comparing with peers
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna was already a legendary warrior but still felt inadequate — achievement doesn't prevent crisis

name: Midlife Crisis
description: Questioning everything at midlife — career, marriage, identity
example_situations: Want to start over but it's too late
arjuna_parallel: The Gita was spoken when Arjuna was in his prime — even peak achievers face existential doubt

name: Exam Anxiety
description: Extreme stress around board exams, JEE, NEET, competitive exams
example_situations: JEE prep destroying my mental health
arjuna_parallel: The battlefield IS the exam hall — Arjuna froze before his biggest test, just like exam panic

name: Academic Failure
description: Failed an important exam or academic pursuit
arjuna_parallel: Krishna tells Arjuna: 'You grieve for those who should not be grieved for' (2.11) — failure is not the end

name: Debt Crisis
description: Overwhelming debt causing constant anxiety
arjuna_parallel: Material attachment and its consequences — Chapter 2 teaches equanimity in loss and gain

name: Sole Earner Burden
description: Entire family financially dependent on one person
example_situations: Whole family depends on my salary
Can't take risks because everyone needs me
arjuna_parallel: The weight of responsibility — Arjuna bore the fate of his entire family on his shoulders

name: Phone/Social Media Addiction
description: Compulsive phone usage destroying productivity and peace
arjuna_parallel: The senses pulling the mind — Chapter 2.67: 'The mind that follows wandering senses carries away wisdom like wind carries a ship on water'

name: Duty vs. Desire
description: Torn between what you should do and what you want to do
example_situations: Should I follow my heart or my responsibility?
Family needs me but I need freedom
arjuna_parallel: This IS Arjuna's exact dilemma — the ENTIRE Gita is about this: duty (dharma) vs personal desire (kama)

name: Why Do Good People Suffer?
description: Questioning cosmic justice — why bad things happen to good people
example_situations: I've been good my whole life, why is this happening?
Corrupt people succeed, honest people suffer
Is there any justice in the world?
arjuna_parallel: The Pandavas were righteous yet exiled for 13 years — the Gita addresses cosmic justice through karma theory

name: Revenge vs. Forgiveness
description: Someone wronged you deeply — struggling between revenge and letting go
example_situations: They destroyed my life, should I forgive?
Want revenge but know it's wrong
arjuna_parallel: The Mahabharata war itself was about this — was revenge justified? Krishna guides Arjuna to act from dharma, not anger

name: Log Kya Kahenge
description: Paralyzed by fear of societal judgment — 'what will people say?'
example_situations: Can't do X because log kya kahenge
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna cared deeply about honor and reputation (2.34-36) — Krishna addresses this directly: 'Those who speak ill of you will slander your great reputation'

name: Uncontrolled Anger
description: Frequent anger outbursts destroying relationships and peace
arjuna_parallel: Chapter 2.62-63: Krishna maps the exact chain — desire → frustration → anger → delusion → destruction. The Gita's anger management is surgical

name: Chronic Self-Doubt
description: Persistent inability to trust oneself or one's abilities
example_situations: Others can do it, not me
arjuna_parallel: Arjuna — the greatest archer — said 'I cannot fight' (1.46). Even the most capable people doubt themselves
